description: Per-study evidence aggregation for the load-bearing platform-thesis claim 'androgens suppress intestinal ABCG2 expression, lowering the dose-response asymptote of the gut-lumen-sink intervention.' Web/PubMed scan 2026-05-07. Includes confirming, contradicting, and ambiguous evidence. Each study is tagged: tissue (intestine/kidney/liver/cell-line), species, intervention (castration/T-replacement/ADT/observational), evidence-tier, direction-of-effect on ABCG2 (relative to androgen elevation), and load-bearing-for-thesis judgment.
search_strategy: PubMed + Google Scholar via WebSearch; targeted searches on (a) castration/orchiectomy + ABCG2 + intestine, (b) sex difference + ABCG2 + duodenum/jejunum, (c) androgen receptor + ABCG2 + promoter, (d) ADT + uric acid + prostate cancer, (e) Q141K + sex difference, (f) FtM transgender + uric acid. Direct PMC/journal WebFetch was 403-blocked; abstracts pulled via WebSearch summarization.

studies.primary_authors: Hoque KM, Halperin Kuhns VL, et al. (Woodward OM, senior)
studies.title: The ABCG2 Q141K hyperuricemia and gout associated variant illuminates the physiology of human urate excretion
studies.species: C57BL/6J mouse, CRISPR knock-in Q140K (orthologue of human Q141K)
studies.intervention: Genetic Q140K vs wild-type x sex stratification
studies.n: not specified in summary; standard mouse-cohort sizes
studies.key_finding_intestinal_abcg2: Male Q140K+/+ mice: total intestinal ABCG2 protein abundance reduced 88% vs wild-type (mRNA unchanged; post-translational defect). Q140K+/− mice: 53% reduction. Female Q140K+/+ mice: NO HYPERURICEMIA, no significant intestinal phenotype despite same genotype. Difference attributed to baseline sex difference in ABCG2 abundance in kidney AND intestine.
studies.magnitude_intestine: 88% reduction protein in male homozygote; 53% in heterozygote (compared to wild-type same-sex baseline). Female Q140K homozygotes: no hyperuricemia, much smaller intestinal effect.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS sex-dimorphism in intestinal ABCG2 — males more vulnerable to ABCG2 loss-of-function. Does NOT directly demonstrate androgen-driven suppression in wild-type baseline (genetic loss-of-function model).
studies.platform_relevance: HIGHEST — single most direct evidence that the intestinal ABCG2 compartment IS sex-dimorphic in a gout-relevant manner. The 88% intestinal protein loss in Q140K+/+ males vs 44% in kidney is the empirical foundation that intestinal ABCG2 is a load-bearing sex-dimorphic compartment.
studies.caveats: (1) Genetic loss-of-function model, NOT wild-type baseline + hormone manipulation. The mechanism for the male-female ABCG2 abundance difference at baseline is INFERRED, not measured. (2) Mouse, not human. (3) Q140K is an ABCG2 mutation; baseline difference between WT males and WT females is implied by the differential phenotype but not quantified as a fold-change of the WT comparison.

studies.title: Influence of androgen deprivation therapy on serum urate levels in patients with prostate cancer: A retrospective observational study
studies.intervention: ADT (LHRH agonist or surgical castration) vs prostatectomy comparison; retrospective observational
studies.key_finding: After 6 months ADT: serum urate decreased −0.66 mg/dL (significant). Surgery group: no significant change. Hyperuricemia prevalence in ADT group dropped from 18.0% baseline to 4.7% at 6 months; surgery group 15.9% to 16.5% (no change).
studies.magnitude_serum_urate: −0.66 mg/dL serum UA on ADT at 6 months
studies.direction_for_thesis: STRONGLY SUPPORTS — androgen ablation lowers serum urate at clinically meaningful magnitude; consistent with androgens being a net urate-elevating signal. Mechanism not directly measured but paper attributes effect to URAT1 (and notes androgen could activate URAT1 promoter activity).
studies.platform_relevance: HIGH — most direct human-cohort evidence that the androgen axis is a real and quantitatively significant lever on serum urate. However, this paper does NOT distinguish renal (URAT1) vs intestinal (ABCG2) mechanisms; the magnitude is consistent with URAT1 alone being the dominant transporter affected.
studies.caveats: (1) Retrospective, not randomized. (2) ADT regimens varied. (3) Mechanism inference (URAT1) is the paper's interpretation; ABCG2 contribution not separately measured. (4) Confounders include weight loss / metabolic changes on ADT.

studies.title: Estradiol regulates intestinal ABCG2 to promote urate excretion via the PI3K/Akt pathway
studies.species: mouse (male hyperuricemic + ovariectomized female) + Caco-2 in vitro
studies.intervention: Estradiol benzoate 8 μg IP every other day (in OVX females and male hyperuricemic mice) + Caco-2 estradiol exposure with LY294002 (PI3K inhibitor)
studies.key_finding: Estradiol benzoate lowered serum urate in both male hyperuricemic mice and OVX females. Estradiol UPREGULATED intestinal ABCG2 expression in vivo and Caco-2 in vitro. PI3K inhibitor LY294002 partially blocked the effect → mechanism is PI3K/Akt-dependent. Hyperuricemia/gout patients had significantly lower estradiol than healthy controls.
studies.magnitude_intestinal_abcg2: Direction confirmed (upregulation). Specific fold-change not extracted from abstract; Western blot in Caco-2 used.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS the platform thesis ASYMMETRICALLY. Confirms that intestinal ABCG2 IS hormone-regulated (PI3K/Akt-mediated by estradiol). The female-protective story is real and primary; the male-suppression story is implied by absence-of-estradiol rather than presence-of-androgen.
studies.platform_relevance: HIGH — best primary-literature anchor for the 'sex hormones modulate intestinal ABCG2' claim. CRITICAL NUANCE: this paper shows estradiol UPregulates intestinal ABCG2 via PI3K/Akt. It does NOT show testosterone DOWNregulates intestinal ABCG2. The sex difference may be ESTRADIOL-driven (positive in females), not ANDROGEN-driven (negative in males) — these have different platform implications (reframe section needed in androgen-urate-axis.md).
studies.caveats: (1) The mechanism shown is positive-female, not negative-male. (2) Generalization to male physiology is mechanistic extrapolation. (3) Caco-2 line.

studies.title: Effect of Sex Hormones on the ABCG2 Transport Protein in Caco-2 Cells
studies.journal: Biochemistry (Moscow), Supplement Series A: Membrane and Cell Biology
studies.intervention: Progesterone, estradiol, testosterone at 1 / 10 / 100 μM, 24h incubation; receptor-antagonist co-treatment for mechanism (PXR, FXR, CAR antagonists)
studies.key_finding: ALL THREE HORMONES (testosterone INCLUDED) at all concentrations tested INCREASED ABCG2 protein content in Caco-2 cells over 24h. Mechanism: testosterone acts via FXR + PXR (not AR directly); PXR/FXR antagonism partially blocked the increase. Progesterone via PXR/FXR; estradiol via CAR/PXR.
studies.magnitude_caco2: Direction: testosterone INCREASES intestinal ABCG2 in this in vitro model. Specific fold-change not in abstract.
studies.direction_for_thesis: DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS the platform thesis. In Caco-2 (the standard intestinal-epithelium model), testosterone is an ABCG2 INDUCER, not suppressor. Mechanism is xenobiotic-receptor-mediated (PXR/FXR), not classical androgen receptor.
studies.platform_relevance: HIGHEST CONTRARIAN — single most direct contradiction of the platform thesis at the intestinal-epithelial level. Caco-2 has limitations as a male/in-vivo model, but the directional finding directly contradicts 'androgens suppress intestinal ABCG2' at face value.
studies.caveats: (1) Caco-2 is a human colon adenocarcinoma cell line — proliferating, not differentiated enterocyte; receptor-expression profile may not reflect normal small intestinal villus. (2) Concentrations tested (1-100 μM testosterone) are supraphysiological; physiological free T is ~0.01-0.05 μM. (3) Androgen receptor (AR) was not directly antagonized — the FXR/PXR mechanism is for the supraphysiological-T xenobiotic-sensor effect, not necessarily the physiological-T endogenous signal. (4) 24h acute, not chronic; not an in vivo confirmation.

studies.primary_authors: Tanaka Y, Slitt AL, Leazer TM, Maher JM, Klaassen CD
studies.title: Tissue distribution and hormonal regulation of the breast cancer resistance protein (Bcrp/Abcg2) in rats and mice
studies.intervention: Gonadectomy + hypophysectomy; tissue mRNA + Northern blot
studies.n: standard cohort sizes (not extracted from summary)
studies.key_finding: Rat Bcrp mRNA: high in intestine and male kidney. Mouse Bcrp: highest in kidney, then liver, ileum, testes. MALE-PREDOMINANT EXPRESSION in rat KIDNEY and mouse LIVER — but the mechanism is suppressive estradiol, NOT inductive testosterone. Gonadectomy/hormone-replacement experiments showed estradiol SUPPRESSES Bcrp in rat kidney; testosterone has minimal direct effect on the male-predominant pattern in rat kidney (i.e., the female-low pattern is estradiol-driven, not the male-high pattern testosterone-driven).
studies.magnitude: Direction asymmetric (estradiol-suppressive in renal/hepatic). Specific magnitudes in original paper.
studies.direction_for_thesis: MIXED. Confirms male > female ABCG2 in rat kidney + mouse liver (NOT specifically intestine — the intestinal segment has high baseline expression in males but no clear male-predominant magnitude reported in the abstract summary). Mechanism = estradiol suppression of female, NOT testosterone suppression of male.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — this is the foundational rodent tissue-distribution paper showing ABCG2 IS hormone-regulated. The directional asymmetry (estradiol-suppressive) reframes the question: the wiki claim 'androgens suppress intestinal ABCG2' should be reframed as 'estradiol DRIVES the female ABCG2-elevated phenotype; absence of estradiol in males explains the lower intestinal ABCG2.' Different framing → different platform implications (estrogen-rescue stack instead of anti-androgen stack).
studies.caveats: (1) Old paper (2005); pre-Q141K-era. (2) Rodent. (3) Intestinal-specific magnitude not separately reported in available summary.

studies.title: Gender differences in mRNA expression of ATP-binding cassette efflux and bile acid transporters in kidney, liver, and intestine of 5/6 nephrectomized rats
studies.key_finding: Male remnant kidneys: kidney-predominant ABCG2 DECREASED with CKD severity. Females: ABCG2 changes largely absent. Mechanism: associated with cytokine increases + decreased ER-α and glucocorticoid receptor — sex-hormone-modulated. Intestinal ABCG2 patterns in the paper are not the headline finding (kidney + liver are).
studies.magnitude_intestine: Not separately reported in available summary.
studies.direction_for_thesis: INDIRECT — confirms sex-stratified ABCG2 regulation in disease state. Does NOT directly probe wild-type baseline intestinal sex difference under hormone manipulation.
studies.platform_relevance: LOW — disease-model context; intestinal arm not the headline.
studies.caveats: (1) Disease state, not healthy baseline. (2) Mechanism via inflammation + ER-α (not direct AR).

studies.title: Functional cyclic AMP response element in the BCRP/ABCG2 promoter modulates EGFR pathway- or androgen withdrawal-mediated BCRP/ABCG2 transcription in human cancer cells
studies.intervention: Androgen withdrawal in LNCaP (charcoal-stripped serum) + CREB/CRTC2 silencing
studies.key_finding: Androgen withdrawal in LNCaP (prostate cancer line) INCREASES BCRP expression. Mechanism: androgen withdrawal → ↑CREB phosphorylation + ↑CRTC2 nuclear translocation → ↑BCRP transcription via the −329 CRE element on the BCRP promoter. NO direct ARE (androgen response element) on the BCRP promoter has been identified. The mechanism is INDIRECT — androgens normally suppress CREB/CRTC2 activity, and removing androgen relieves this suppression.
studies.magnitude_lncap: Direction: androgen withdrawal → BCRP increase. Magnitude: not extracted from abstract.
studies.direction_for_thesis: MECHANISTICALLY SUPPORTS — provides molecular mechanism by which androgens could indirectly suppress BCRP/ABCG2 transcription (via tonic CREB/CRTC2 inhibition). However, this is in PROSTATE CANCER CELLS, not enterocytes. Whether the same CREB/CRTC2 mechanism operates in intestinal epithelium is not demonstrated.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — best mechanism-level molecular story for indirect androgen → ABCG2 suppression. Receptor mechanism: NOT direct AR-ARE binding; INDIRECT via CREB/CRTC2 axis. Tissue context (prostate cancer) limits direct extrapolation to intestine.
studies.caveats: (1) Cancer cell line, not normal enterocyte. (2) Prostate context — androgen-AR signaling is pharmacologically central to LNCaP biology in a way that may not generalize. (3) Direction confirmed; magnitude not extracted; whether the magnitude in normal intestine would be clinically meaningful is unknown.

studies.primary_authors: Yahyaoui R, T'Sjoen G, et al.
studies.title: Effect of long-term administration of cross-sex hormone therapy on serum and urinary uric acid in transsexual persons
studies.species: human (47 FtM, 22 MtF; cross-sex hormone therapy)
studies.intervention: FtM: testosterone esters; MtF: estrogen + anti-androgen. 2-year follow-up.
studies.key_finding: FtM (T administration): serum urate ROSE significantly; FEUA (renal fractional excretion of urate) DECREASED. MtF (estrogen + anti-androgen): serum urate FELL; FEUA INCREASED. Authors: 'no clear link between testosterone and FEUA' in their adjusted analysis — the effect attribution is to estrogen-driven FEUA increase in MtF, not directly to testosterone-driven FEUA decrease in FtM (the latter is implied but the FEUA relationship is symmetric, not causally locked to T).
studies.magnitude_serum_urate: FtM 2-year urate rise significant; specific mg/dL not extracted.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS the androgen → urate-rise direction; mechanism interpretation favors the RENAL FEUA path (URAT1, GLUT9), not extrarenal (intestinal ABCG2). The paper itself does not measure intestinal ABCG2 or fecal urate.
studies.platform_relevance: HIGH for the broad androgen-urate axis claim; LOW for intestinal-ABCG2-specifically. Clinical-cohort confirmation that adding testosterone to baseline-female (FtM) physiology raises urate via renal fractional-excretion change.
studies.caveats: (1) FtM cohort starts at female-physiology baseline; supraphysiological-feminine, not physiological-male, so the direction-of-effect is real but the magnitude in male-physiology baseline may differ. (2) Mechanism inferred renal — paper notes no clear T → FEUA causal link in adjusted analysis. (3) Intestinal ABCG2 not measured.

studies.title: Sex Differences in Urate Handling
studies.key_finding: Review acknowledges female-protective sex difference in urate. Highlights ABCG2 as the most sex-dimorphic transporter in clinical effect (ABCG2 risk allele effect amplified in males; SLC2A9 risk allele effect amplified in females). For the mechanism: the review acknowledges the question is OPEN; testosterone direct effect on intestinal ABCG2 is not pinned down. Reviews Q141K mouse work and the renal vs intestinal compartment difference. Acknowledges the female-baseline-protected pattern is the dominant evidence direction.
studies.direction_for_thesis: MIXED — review confirms the sex-dimorphic pattern at the population level but does NOT confirm a direct androgen-suppression mechanism on intestinal ABCG2. The wiki's androgen-suppression framing is more specific than the review supports.
studies.platform_relevance: HIGH — most authoritative recent review on the question. Should be the primary citation in the wiki for the sex-dimorphism claim, with explicit acknowledgment that the intestinal-androgen-direct-suppression arm is OPEN.
studies.caveats: Review-level — useful for framing but not for primary-mechanism evidence.

studies.title: Closing the gaps: a full scan of the intestinal expression of P-glycoprotein, breast cancer resistance protein, and multidrug resistance-associated protein 2 in male and female rats
studies.intervention: Sex stratification (M vs F), full intestinal scan in 3-cm segments
studies.n: n=10 per sex (typical for paper of this type)
studies.key_finding: BCRP/ABCG2 showed an arcuate pattern with highest expression toward end of small intestine. NO GENDER-SPECIFIC DIFFERENCES could be observed for ABCG2 (or P-gp / MRP2). Wide intra- and interindividual variability. The paper's headline finding for sex is NULL.
studies.magnitude: No significant sex difference in intestinal ABCG2 protein abundance in healthy Wistar rats.
studies.direction_for_thesis: DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS the platform thesis at the level of healthy-baseline sex difference in rat intestine. The wiki's 'intestinal ABCG2 expression does not differ markedly by sex' summary that the verification subagent flagged is consistent with this paper.
studies.platform_relevance: HIGHEST CONTRARIAN — strongest direct intestinal-tissue rodent evidence that male-female ABCG2 abundance difference is NOT detectable at baseline (in healthy non-disease state, no hormone manipulation). This is the strongest single argument against the 'structural ceiling' framing.
studies.caveats: (1) Rat, not human. (2) Healthy state — does not preclude that disease/inflammation/genotype amplifies a latent sex difference. (3) Methodology = whole-tissue homogenate; could miss apical-membrane-specific differences. (4) Wistar strain — not all rat strains may show same pattern.

studies.title: Protein abundance of clinically relevant multidrug transporters along the entire length of the human intestine
studies.key_finding: ABCG2 protein abundance highest in jejunum and ileum; lower in colon. Did NOT report a significant sex difference in ABCG2 expression — sex was a covariate, not a primary analysis dimension. Wide interindividual variability dominated.
studies.magnitude: No reported significant sex difference; n likely too small to power a sex-stratified analysis.
studies.direction_for_thesis: NEUTRAL / WEAK CONTRARIAN — best human-intestinal-proteomics dataset available. Does not detect a sex difference, but power to detect one is limited.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — most direct human evidence; underpowered for sex stratification.
studies.caveats: (1) Small n. (2) Post-mortem / surgical residual = potential disease confounding. (3) Sex stratification not pre-specified primary analysis.

studies.primary_authors: Hosoyamada M, Takiue Y, et al.
studies.title: The effect of testosterone upon the urate reabsorptive transport system in mouse kidney
studies.key_finding: Orchiectomy reduces URAT1 mRNA + protein in male mouse kidney; testosterone replacement restores. T enhances Smct1 (mRNA + protein) and represses Glut9. URAT1 mRNA is enhanced by T but URAT1 PROTEIN level was unaffected by T in non-orchiectomized animals — Smct1 induction is the proximate mechanism. ABCG2 NOT MEASURED in this paper (focused on URAT1, GLUT9, SMCT1).
studies.direction_for_thesis: TANGENTIAL — confirms androgen → URAT1 axis (renal); does NOT inform intestinal ABCG2.
studies.platform_relevance: LOW for intestinal ABCG2 question; HIGH for the renal-URAT1 arm of the axis (which is the better-supported half).
studies.caveats: ABCG2 explicitly not in this paper's panel.

studies.title: The effect of female hormones upon urate transport systems in the mouse kidney
studies.key_finding: Estradiol SUPPRESSES URAT1, GLUT9, AND renal ABCG2 in mouse kidney. Note: this is RENAL ABCG2 going DOWN under estradiol, opposite-direction from S03_Yu2021 INTESTINAL ABCG2 going UP under estradiol — confirms tissue-compartment-specific opposite directionality of estradiol on ABCG2.
studies.direction_for_thesis: MIXED. Shows tissue-compartment-specific opposite-direction of estradiol on ABCG2 (renal down, intestinal up). Reinforces that compartment-specific framing is essential — this is the strongest evidence for the principle that 'renal ABCG2 ≠ intestinal ABCG2' in hormone regulation.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — important for the framing principle. Does not directly confirm androgen → intestinal ABCG2 effect.
studies.caveats: Renal not intestinal; estradiol not testosterone.

studies.title: Menopause, postmenopausal hormone use and serum uric acid levels in US women — NHANES III
studies.key_finding: Postmenopausal +0.34 mg/dL urate vs premenopausal. HRT users −0.24 mg/dL adjusted (−0.44 unadjusted). Women's UA rises toward male range with menopause.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS the female-protective pattern (estrogen-driven). Indirectly relevant — magnitudes are with intestinal ABCG2 contribution being one of multiple possible mechanisms (not directly measured).
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — supports the female-protective framing; does not specifically isolate intestinal ABCG2 as the mechanism.

studies.title: Changes in uric acid metabolism and associated plasma proteomics during sex hormone therapy
studies.key_finding: Testosterone in transgender males significantly increased uric acid; FEUA significantly fell. Effect dose-dependent. Mechanisms cited: reduced estrogen → decreased urinary urate excretion (renal); plus increased muscle mass (production). Intestinal ABCG2 not directly measured; the authors do not invoke an ABCG2 mechanism.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS — adding testosterone raises urate. Mechanism inferred RENAL (FEUA fall), not intestinal.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — extends Yahyaoui 2008 to a more recent / larger cohort. Intestinal ABCG2 still not measured.

studies.title: Establishment of sex difference in circulating uric acid is associated with higher testosterone and lower SHBG in adolescent boys
studies.key_finding: Sex difference in circulating uric acid is established during adolescence as a result of a dramatic increase in uric acid in adolescent boys, correlated with higher testosterone and lower SHBG. Estradiol not inversely associated with UA after full adjustment in this analysis.
studies.direction_for_thesis: SUPPORTS — testosterone is the longitudinal driver of male-elevated UA in puberty. Mechanism not measured at tissue level.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — confirms T as a real urate-driver in human male physiology.

studies.primary_authors: Halperin Kuhns VL et al. (Woodward lab)
studies.title: Differential expression of renal urate transporters in male and female mice (companion FASEB abstract to Hoque 2020)
studies.key_finding: Female mice have LOWER transcript expression of urate transporters SLC22A12 (URAT1), SLC22A6 (OAT1), SLC17A1, ABCA1 — RENAL. AND lower HNF1A, HNF4A, HNF4G transcription factors. Females may have more efficient urate excretion. (Renal stratification, not intestinal.)
studies.direction_for_thesis: MIXED — RENAL transporter sex difference is documented. The paper emphasizes female-low-transporter as the mechanism, NOT male-high-driven-by-T.
studies.platform_relevance: MEDIUM — same lab as the more cited Hoque 2020 paper. Reframes the question: maybe sex difference in urate is partly a transcription-factor (HNF) network difference, not a direct hormone-driven transporter regulation.

supports_intestinal_sex_dimorphism_M_low: S01_Hoque2020 (genetic LoF model — males more vulnerable)
contradicts_T_suppresses_intestinal_ABCG: S05_Tanaka2005 (mechanism = estradiol-suppressive of female, NOT T-suppressive of male)
supports_axis_via_androgens_indirectly: S02_Sakamoto2018 (ADT lowers UA, mechanism not isolated)

verdict_logic.claim_being_tested: Androgens (testosterone, DHT) directly suppress INTESTINAL ABCG2 expression at magnitudes consistent with a structural ceiling on the gut-lumen-sink platform efficacy in male/androgen-dominant patients.
verdict_logic.evidence_for_direct_T_suppression_intest: ZERO primary studies demonstrate this directly (no paper shows: castration of healthy male animal → increased intestinal ABCG2, OR T administration → decreased intestinal ABCG2). The Jeong 2015 LNCaP study shows mechanism in prostate cancer cells but does not extend to intestine.
verdict_logic.evidence_against_direct_T_suppression_in: Klyushova 2023 (Caco-2: T INCREASES ABCG2). MacLean 2008 (rat full intestinal scan: no sex difference). Tanaka 2005 (mechanism is estradiol-suppressive of females, not T-suppressive of males).
verdict_logic.evidence_for_intestinal_compartment_bein: Hoque 2020 (Q140K male hyperuricemia + 88% intestinal protein loss only in males); Yu 2021 (estradiol upregulates intestinal ABCG2 — implying females have higher baseline driven by estradiol); FtM cohort data (T raises serum UA but mechanism inferred renal).
verdict_logic.summary_judgment: WEAK / UNCONFIRMED — the SPECIFIC claim that androgens directly suppress intestinal ABCG2 is not supported by primary literature. The BROADER claim that intestinal ABCG2 is sex-dimorphic in a urate-relevant way IS supported. The mechanism appears to be ESTRADIOL POSITIVE in females rather than ANDROGEN NEGATIVE in males. The structural-ceiling argument needs to be SOFTENED but is not entirely wrong — it should be reframed as 'males lack the estradiol-driven intestinal ABCG2 induction that females have, so the gut-lumen-sink asymptote is lower in males than females' rather than 'androgens actively suppress intestinal ABCG2.'
